summary:
AI coding agents do more than edit files. They follow prompts, talk to other agents, run tests, inspect logs, retry commands, skip failures, touch the filesystem, and make decisions that never appear in the commit.

**h5i turns every AI coding session into a Git-native receipt** — a scored prompt, model provenance, token-reduced command evidence, recoverable raw logs, agent-to-agent discussion, a confined sandbox, and a reviewer-ready PR brief. All of it versioned in your own repo and carried by `h5i share push`/`pull`. No SaaS, no new VCS, no lock-in.

> [!NOTE]
> 🌳 **Prompt maturity: 81/100** · _advanced_ · 7 prompts scored (100% of AI commits)
> 🔧 _Recurring weak spots: weak context._
> _Heuristic signal of prompt craft — not a developer rating._

Every AI-authored PR arrives with a receipt that leads with the score above, then attaches the model, the reasoning, the tests, the captured command output, and the risk flags. **GitHub tells you _what_ changed. h5i tells you whether the work was responsibly delegated, observed, tested, and reviewable.**

… [87 lines elided] …

## 3. Feature Examples

The spine is **a scored prompt and a reviewer-ready receipt** for every AI-authored change. Everything else — agent radio, the sandbox, token reduction, the context DAG — feeds that record.

### 3.1. Prompt Maturity Score — make prompt craft a visible signal

h5i already records the prompt behind every AI commit. The **Prompt Maturity Score** turns those prompts into a single, explainable **0–100** signal of *how well the work was delegated* — computed **fully offline**: no LLM, no network, deterministic enough to run in a Git hook, in CI, or in a PR render with no API key.

It scores the **input** — the engineer's ask — not the model's output, as a weighted sum of seven classical-NLP signals:

| Signal | Weight | Captures |

7: 18:{"@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "PresentationDigitalDocument", "name": "h5i Pitch Deck", "headline": "h5i Pitch Deck — AI-Aware Version Control for Agent Work", "description": "A Git sidecar that gives AI-authored code a reviewable evidence layer — provenance on every commit, cross-agent messaging, a token-saving object store, reviewer-ready PRs, and deterministic audit.", "url": "https://h5i.dev/pitch/", "inLanguage": "en", "author": {"@type": "Person", "name": "Koukyosyumei", "url": "https://github.com/Koukyosyumei"}, "publisher": {"@type": "Organization", "name": "h5i", "logo": {"@type": "ImageObject", "url": "https://h5i.dev/_static/logo.png"}}, "about": {"@type": "SoftwareApplication", "name": "h5i", "applicationCategory": "DeveloperApplication"}} 25: 32: 150: .section-tag { 156: .section-tag::before { 548: /* ── Prompt-score bars ───────────────────────────────────── */ 591:
50 years of version control
592:

Every era needed a new tool.
The AI era is no different.

616:
Git
618:
Git
642:
The problem
643:

Code review lost the execution trail
when agents arrived.

644:

An agent opens a PR. Before a human can approve it, six questions need answers Git was never built to store.

658: SLIDE — Why now 662:
Why now
663:

AI writes a growing share of code.
Review capacity doesn't scale with it.

664:

Agents already author production diffs at machine speed. The work behind them — prompts, commands, reasoning, test runs — evaporates the moment a session ends. Every team adopting agents is quietly accumulating change that is unreviewable, unauditable, and unreproducible.

668:

Flat review hours

Humans still approve every merge — with the same time and the same thin context Git gives them.

679:
The solution
680:

An evidence layer that lives
beside every Git branch

681:

h5i is a Git sidecar. Your commits, branches, and remotes stay exactly as they are — h5i writes the AI context to its own refs/h5i/* refs that travel with the repo.

685:
refs/h5i/contextversioned reasoning trace — OBSERVE / THINK / ACT, branched like Git
689:
your Git repocommits, branches, any remote — unchanged. No SaaS, no new VCS, no lock-in.
695: SLIDE — The wedge · Prompt Maturity Score 699:
The wedge · Prompt Maturity Score
700:

Prompt quality becomes a
measurable, reviewable signal

746:
Feature · h5i capture commit
747:

Every AI commit ships
with its provenance

788:
Feature · h5i msg
789:

Claude and Codex coordinate
through Git

832:
Feature · h5i capture run
833:

Tool output stops eating
the context window

867:
Feature · h5i share pr
868:

The PR becomes the
reviewer's cockpit

878:
Prompt maturity81/100 · advanced · 7 prompts scored
887:

One command renders everything h5i knows about the branch into a sticky, idempotent GitHub PR comment — so a human reviewer sees the context, not just the diff.

908:
Feature · h5i audit
909:

Deterministic risk triage —
no model in the loop

946:
Feature · h5i env
947:

A confined, fully auditable
agent sandbox

977: SLIDE 9 — Why now / proof 981:
Market & moat
982:

Drops into the stack every
AI team already runs

983:

No migration. h5i sits on top of Git, PRs, and the agents teams already use — and every session it observes compounds into a private, content-addressed record that is hard to leave behind.

992:
structured traces

Every session compounds a private corpus of how the team delegates to agents.

993:
Git-native distribution

Ships through the remotes teams already have — no SaaS to adopt, no data to hand over.

994:
deterministic & offline

Prompt + risk scoring nothing can replicate without your repo. Apache-2.0 Rust core.

1006:

Version control that
remembers why

1007:

Git shows the diff. h5i shows the agent work behind it.

1008:

A Git sidecar — a scored prompt and provenance on every commit, agents that coordinate, tool output that doesn't bloat context, PRs a human can actually review. Without replacing Git.

summary:
README.md:8: README.md:10: README.md:13: README.md:14: README.md:21:AI coding agents do more than edit files. They follow prompts, talk to other agents, run tests, inspect logs, retry commands, skip failures, touch the filesystem, and make decisions that never appear in the commit. README.md:23:**h5i turns every AI coding session into a Git-native receipt** — a scored prompt, model provenance, token-reduced command evidence, recoverable raw logs, agent-to-agent discussion, a confined sandbox, and a reviewer-ready PR brief. All of it versioned in your own repo and carried by `h5i share push`/`pull`. No SaaS, no new VCS, no lock-in. README.md:26:> 🌳 **Prompt maturity: 81/100** · _advanced_ · 7 prompts scored (100% of AI commits) README.md:28:> _Heuristic signal of prompt craft — not a developer rating._ README.md:30:Every AI-authored PR arrives with a receipt that leads with the score above, then attaches the model, the reasoning, the tests, the captured command output, and the risk flags. **GitHub tells you _what_ changed. h5i tells you whether the work was responsibly delegated, observed, tested, and reviewable.** README.md:36: Offline prompt score README.md:55:- Want an objective, offline score for how well each commit's prompt was written — trended across the whole branch? README.md:56:- Want to know which model, prompt, and reasoning led to a commit? … [78 lines elided] … docs/index.html:878: h5i (pronounced high-five) captures that missing execution layer as Git-backed evidence: prompts and model metadata, token-reduced command output, recoverable raw logs, a sandboxed worktree, real-time multi-agent discussion, and review-ready context for PRs. … [32 lines elided] … docs/index.html:1289: --agent codex --prompt "..." docs/index.html:1315:
h5i (pronounced high-five) is an open-source Git sidecar for AI-era development. Git records what changed; h5i records the rest — the prompt, model, agent, reasoning, file observations, decisions, audit signals, and memory behind every change — and versions it alongside your code in refs/h5i/*.
docs/index.html:1319:
Yes. h5i is built for teams where AI agents write code alongside humans. Claude Code integrates via hooks and MCP; Codex uses explicit prelude, sync, and finish commands. Both agents restore the same shared context when a session starts and check their work back in when it ends.
docs/index.html:1327:
A deterministic 0–100 score for the prompt behind a commit, rolled up across a branch and rendered at the top of the PR brief. It rewards prompts that name the goal, set constraints, reference real files, and ask for verification — computed fully offline from text features (no LLM, no API key), so it's free on every commit, reproducible in CI, and explainable point-by-point. It scores the engineer's input, not the model's output, and is a heuristic signal of prompt craft — not a developer rating.
docs/index.html:1330: Why does an AI-heavy team need this? docs/index.html:1331:
AI agents now produce a growing share of production diffs, but review and compliance budgets don't scale with them. h5i is the audit trail for AI coding sessions: every change carries who/which-model authored it, how well it was prompted, what commands ran, whether tests passed, and what risks were flagged — versioned in your own Git repo, shareable with push/pull, no SaaS. It makes agent work reproducible, reviewable, and accountable without adding a new system of record.
docs/index.html:1342: Can h5i detect prompt injection in AI-generated code? docs/index.html:1343:
h5i audit scan applies deterministic regex rules to every OBSERVE/THINK/ACT entry in an agent's reasoning trace to flag prompt-injection signals — with no model in the audit path — and can rank which commits most need human review.
docs/index.html:1361: Star on GitHub docs/index.html:1362: Read Workflows docs/index.html:1377: GitHub docs/index.html:1380: Workflows
highlights: README.md:21:AI coding agents do more than edit files. They follow prompts, talk to other agents, run tests, inspect logs, retry commands, skip failures, touch the filesystem, and make decisions that never appear in the commit.

docs/index.html:878: h5i (pronounced high-five) captures that missing execution layer as Git-backed evidence: prompts and model metadata, token-reduced command output, recoverable raw logs, a sandboxed worktree, real-time multi-agent discussion, and review-ready context for PRs.

A test or build log can be megabytes. The agent needs the two failures and the count — not the noise. h5i stores raw output out-of-band and surfaces a tiny structured summary.

h5i capture run -- cargo test

Wrap any command. Failures always captured & searchable.

h5i recall objects --status failed

Query findings across every capture — "did this fail before?"

+ What is the Prompt Maturity Score? +
A deterministic 0–100 score for the prompt behind a commit, rolled up across a branch and rendered at the top of the PR brief. It rewards prompts that name the goal, set constraints, reference real files, and ask for verification — computed fully offline from text features (no LLM, no API key), so it's free on every commit, reproducible in CI, and explainable point-by-point. It scores the engineer's input, not the model's output, and is a heuristic signal of prompt craft — not a developer rating.
+
+
+ Why does an AI-heavy team need this?

summary:
AI coding agents do more than edit files. They follow prompts, talk to other agents, run tests, inspect logs, retry commands, skip failures, touch the filesystem, and make decisions that never appear in the commit. h5i captures that missing execution layer as Git-backed evidence: prompts and model metadata, token-reduced command output, recoverable raw logs, sandboxed worktree environment, real-time multi-agent discussion, and review-ready context for PRs.
Git-native

One constraint: if keeping current narrative, the adopted sections must carry more weight. Beachhead line should be explicit: platform/security/DevEx teams rolling out Claude/Codex in GitHub orgs.

Also fix my bad numbers in any draft: use verified 256 refs/h5i/msg messages, ~28 branch objects, ~96 reasoning milestones, and this Claude↔Codex rewrite coordination. Good call not to repeat 387/960 if they are repo-wide or context-index artifacts rather than current-branch proof." && h5i codex sync

/home/koukyosyumei/Dev/h5i/docs/blog/sandboxing-ai-agents-foundations/index.html:73: An AI coding agent is a strange security subject. It is not exactly malware, because you invited it in. It is not exactly a normal developer tool, because it reads untrusted project text and then chooses commands. It is not exactly a compiler, because it can browse your repository, run package managers, inspect environment variables, invoke shells, and decide that the next step is a network request. Sandboxing is the discipline of making that power conditional — and it is the confinement pillar of an auditable workspace: the place where you can say the agent physically could not reach certain resources, provably. /home/koukyosyumei/Dev/h5i/docs/blog/sandboxing-ai-agents-foundations/index.html:254:

/home/koukyosyumei/Dev/h5i/docs/blog/sandboxing-ai-agents-implementation/index.html:74: A sandbox implementation is not one mechanism. It is a launch protocol. You prepare a filesystem view, create namespaces, drop privileges, install syscall policy, install resource limits, configure networking, start the workload, observe the workload, and preserve enough evidence to review the result. The order matters because a process that runs before the boundary is complete is not confined by the boundary. summary:
Why now

Agents ship at machine speed.
Review and audit don't scale with them.

Agents already author production diffs and do unreviewed operational work. The run behind them evaporates when a session ends, every team adopting agents is quietly accumulating change that is unreviewable, unauditable, and unreproducible. h5i drops onto the Git, PRs, and agents teams already run, with no migration.

UP TO95%less token waste, raw logs stay out of context
5evidence refs that travel with the repo

summary:
SLIDE 2 — The Problem ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
The problem

Agents do the work.
Git records only the output.

AI agents now do unreviewed operational work, they run commands, inspect logs, retry, coordinate with each other, and touch files long before a human ever opens a PR. Git captures the final diff. The run that produced it is gone.

What prompt produced this change, and was it any good?git can't say
Which commands did the agent run, and what did the logs show?git can't say
Did the tests pass, and which ones?git can't say
What sandbox policy confined it, and what did it try to reach?git can't say
Did another agent review or hand this off, and at what risk?git can't say
The solution

One auditable workspace
per agent task

h5i is a Git sidecar. For every agent task it opens an auditable workspace, a Git-backed worktree where the agent works, and every prompt, decision, command, log, policy, and handoff is recorded in your repo and provable after the fact. No SaaS, no lock-in, works offline.

Workspace = worktree + prompt + model + commands + logs
           + policy + messages + PR evidence
h5i env

A sandboxed worktree the agent works inside, confined by policy, disposable.

refs/h5i/*

The evidence, prompts, logs, messages, policy, versioned beside your code.

h5i share pr

The provable record, surfaced on the PR a human actually reviews.

What "auditable" means

Three properties of
every workspace

Not three products, three things the workspace lets you prove. Each is recorded in your repo and replayable without a model in the loop.

Provenance

Who / why / what it knew

The prompt, model, files read, decisions, and tests behind every commit, written to refs/h5i/notes and refs/h5i/context.

Confinement

What it couldn't reach

A policy-enforced sandbox records every blocked access. "The agent physically could not exfiltrate" beats "we logged it." refs/h5i/env.

Governance

Deterministic audit

Rule-based risk triage and compliance, blind edits, secrets, prompt injection, reproducible in CI, no model in the loop. h5i audit.

Lives in your Git (refs/h5i/*) and travels with the repo, no SaaS, no new VCS, no lock-in.
highlights: A policy-enforced sandbox records every blocked access. "The agent physically could not exfiltrate" beats "we logged it." refs/h5i/env.

structured.body:
SLIDE 2 — The Problem ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
The problem

Agents do the work.
Git records only the output.

AI agents now do unreviewed operational work, they run commands, inspect logs, retry, coordinate with each other, and touch files long before a human ever opens a PR. Git captures the final diff. The run that produced it is gone.

What prompt produced this change, and was it any good?git can't say
Which commands did the agent run, and what did the logs show?git can't say
Did the tests pass, and which ones?git can't say
What sandbox policy confined it, and what did it try to reach?git can't say
Did another agent review or hand this off, and at what risk?git can't say
The solution

One auditable workspace
per agent task

h5i is a Git sidecar. For every agent task it opens an auditable workspace, a Git-backed worktree where the agent works, and every prompt, decision, command, log, policy, and handoff is recorded in your repo and provable after the fact. No SaaS, no lock-in, works offline.

Workspace = worktree + prompt + model + commands + logs
           + policy + messages + PR evidence
h5i env

A sandboxed worktree the agent works inside, confined by policy, disposable.

refs/h5i/*

The evidence, prompts, logs, messages, policy, versioned beside your code.

h5i share pr

The provable record, surfaced on the PR a human actually reviews.

What "auditable" means

Three properties of
every workspace

Not three products, three things the workspace lets you prove. Each is recorded in your repo and replayable without a model in the loop.

Provenance

Who / why / what it knew

The prompt, model, files read, decisions, and tests behind every commit, written to refs/h5i/notes and refs/h5i/context.

Confinement

What it couldn't reach

A policy-enforced sandbox records every blocked access. "The agent physically could not exfiltrate" beats "we logged it." refs/h5i/env.

Governance

Deterministic audit

Rule-based risk triage and compliance, blind edits, secrets, prompt injection, reproducible in CI, no model in the loop. h5i audit.

Lives in your Git (refs/h5i/*) and travels with the repo, no SaaS, no new VCS, no lock-in.

summary:
Pillar · Confinement · the hero

The sandbox is the teeth
behind the audit claim

h5i env hands an agent a disposable, policy-confined worktree, it picks the strongest isolation the host supports (Landlock, namespaces, rootless Podman) and records every command and every blocked access. Nothing reaches your branch until a human applies.

~/my-project 
$ cat .h5i/env.toml
  isolation  = "supervised"
  net.egress = ["example.com"]   # allowlist

$ h5i env shell box
box$ curl https://example.com
  200 OK          — on the allowlist
box$ curl https://evil.example
  could not resolve host — blocked
box$ nft flush ruleset
  Operation not permitted  — can't undo it

Docker and Codespaces give an agent a box. h5i gives it a box wired into Git review, the diff, the policy, and every blocked access land in your repo, and merge is a git-native diff → apply a human drives.

h5i env create · shell · run

Make a confined box, then hand it to an agent. Every command is confined and captured by construction.

h5i env diff · apply

Review what changed and what was blocked, then merge, reviewer-selected, never automatic.

Pillar · Provenance

Every agent commit ships
with its workspace record

~/my-project 
$ git add src/auth/token.rs
$ h5i capture commit -m "fix token refresh" \
    --agent claude --tests --audit

✔ commit 1a2b3c4 · notes → refs/h5i/notes
✔ tests: 142 passed, 0 failed (3.1s)
✔ integrity audit: 12 rules · clean

$ h5i recall log --limit 1
# the diff — now with the why attached

One command turns an agent's edit into a reviewable record, written to refs/h5i/notes, never touching your source tree.

prompt"harden refresh window"
modelclaude-sonnet-4-6 · 8.4k tokens
agentclaude-code
readtoken.rs, session.rs, 2 more
tests142 passed · 0 failed
auditclean · 12 deterministic rules
highlights: tests142 passed · 0 failed

summary:
Product demo · the golden path

Open a workspace →
hand it to an agent → ship the record

~/my-project 
$ h5i env create fix-auth
  worktree + policy ready
$ h5i env shell fix-auth      # agent works in the box
$ h5i capture run -- cargo test
  142 passed · raw log out-of-band
$ h5i msg review codex --focus token.rs
  → codex re-reviewed, flagged cache scope, DONE
$ h5i share pr
  ✔ PR #42 — evidence brief posted
$ h5i env apply fix-auth   # human merges

One path, end to end: env create → agent works → capture run → msg review → share pr → apply. Evidence accrues at every step; the reviewer reads a provable record on the PR; a human applies the merge.

h5i msg review · risk · handoff

Agents coordinate through Git

Claude and Codex share one repo and a typed channel, ask, review, risk, handoff, done, stored append-only in refs/h5i/msg. No server, no daemon.

h5i capture run -- <cmd>

Logs without the bloat

Raw tool output stays out-of-band and recoverable, only a tiny structured summary lands in context. Up to 95% less token waste.

The moat

summary:
SLIDE — Why now ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════ -->
Why now

Agents ship at machine speed.
Review and audit don't scale with them.

Agents already author production diffs and do unreviewed operational work. The run behind them evaporates when a session ends, every team adopting agents is quietly accumulating change that is unreviewable, unauditable, and unreproducible. h5i drops onto the Git, PRs, and agents teams already run, with no migration.

UP TO95%less token waste, raw logs stay out of context
5evidence refs that travel with the repo
0SaaS, daemons, or migrations to adopt
more agent diffs

A rising share of every PR is machine-authored, and it only grows.

Git-native distribution

Ships through the remotes teams already have, with no SaaS.

rising accountability

Security, compliance, and provenance demands climb as AI becomes production labor.

Built with h5i: this repo develops itself through h5i, cross-agent messages, reasoning milestones, and this pitch + docs rewritten by Claude and Codex coordinating over h5i msg.

Workspaceenv/fix-auth · supervised sandbox · 0 policy violations

One command renders everything h5i knows about the branch into a sticky, idempotent GitHub PR comment, so a human reviewer sees the context, not just the diff.

h5i share pr post --style review

h5i share pr body --style review

Render anywhere

summary:
git diff: 1 file changed, +80 -81 diff --git a/docs/pitch/index.html b/docs/pitch/index.html index 148b6ea0..d07417a7 100644 --- a/docs/pitch/index.html +++ b/docs/pitch/index.html @@ -3,8 +3,8 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -h5i Pitch Deck: Auditable Workspaces for AI Agents - +h5i Pitch Deck: Git-Native Evidence for AI Agent Work + … [3 lines elided] … @@ -588,9 +588,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Auditable workspaces for AI agents
-

Git tracks the diff.
h5i tracks the workspace behind it.

-

50 years of version control got better at recording the diff. AI agents don't just write diffs, they run commands, read logs, retry, and coordinate. The era's answer is the auditable workspace: a Git-backed worktree where every prompt, command, log, and policy is provable after the fact.

+
Git-native evidence for AI agent work
+

AI changes the unit of software work.
Git needs a sidecar.

+

Git was built for human-authored diffs. Agents create execution: prompts, tool calls, logs, retries, blocked accesses, handoffs, and tests. h5i preserves that execution history in Git so teams can review AI work with evidence instead of faith.

+

… [3 lines elided] … @@ -617,7 +617,7 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -

+
Distributed diffs
… [3 lines elided] … @@ -627,7 +627,7 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Auditable workspaces for AI agents
+
Version control for agent execution
… [3 lines elided] … @@ -640,16 +640,16 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
The problem
-

Agents do the work.
Git records only the output.

-

AI agents now do unreviewed operational work, they run commands, inspect logs, retry, coordinate with each other, and touch files long before a human ever opens a PR. Git captures the final diff. The run that produced it is gone.

+
The breakage
+

The diff lands.
The work that produced it vanishes.

+

An agent can inspect sensitive code, run commands, fail tests, retry with a different strategy, coordinate with another agent, and then leave behind only a patch. That is not enough for security, platform, or DevEx teams asked to approve AI-written code.

… [15 lines elided] … @@ -659,21 +659,21 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
The solution
-

One auditable workspace
per agent task

-

h5i is a Git sidecar. For every agent task it opens an auditable workspace, a Git-backed worktree where the agent works, and every prompt, decision, command, log, policy, and handoff is recorded in your repo and provable after the fact. No SaaS, no lock-in, works offline.

+
The product
+

h5i gives each agent task
an auditable workspace.

+

A workspace is the missing review object: a disposable worktree plus the prompt, model, commands, logs, policy, messages, claims, and PR evidence that explain how the diff came to exist.

… [20 lines elided] … @@ -683,29 +683,27 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
What "auditable" means
-

Three properties of
every workspace

-

Not three products, three things the workspace lets you prove. Each is recorded in your repo and replayable without a model in the loop.

+
The wedge
+

Start where the pain is acute:
reviewing AI-written PRs.

+

Teams do not need another agent UI. They need a trustworthy receipt when an agent asks to merge code. h5i starts as the receipt layer for platform, security, and DevEx owners who must make agent work governable without slowing it to human speed.

… [32 lines elided] … @@ -714,9 +712,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Pillar · Confinement · the hero
-

The sandbox is the teeth
behind the audit claim

-

h5i env hands an agent a disposable, policy-confined worktree, it picks the strongest isolation the host supports (Landlock, namespaces, rootless Podman) and records every command and every blocked access. Nothing reaches your branch until a human applies.

+
Confinement
+

The sandbox makes the receipt
believable.

+

h5i env hands an agent a disposable, policy-confined worktree, picks the strongest isolation the host supports, and records every command plus every blocked access. Nothing reaches your branch until a human applies it.

… [3 lines elided] … @@ -737,9 +735,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -

Docker and Codespaces give an agent a box. h5i gives it a box wired into Git review, the diff, the policy, and every blocked access land in your repo, and merge is a git-native diff → apply a human drives.

-
h5i env create · shell · run

Make a confined box, then hand it to an agent. Every command is confined and captured by construction.

-
h5i env diff · apply

Review what changed and what was blocked, then merge, reviewer-selected, never automatic.

+

Docker and Codespaces give an agent a box. h5i gives it a box wired into Git review, so the diff, the policy, and every blocked access land in the repository together.

+
h5i env create / shell / run

Make a confined task workspace, then hand it to an agent. Commands are captured by construction.

+
h5i env diff / apply

Review what changed and what was blocked, then merge only the reviewer-selected patch.

Pillar · Provenance
-

Every agent commit ships
with its workspace record

+

+

+h5i Pitch Deck: Git-Native Evidence for AI Agent Work + … [3 lines elided] … @@ -15,21 +15,21 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -{"@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "PresentationDigitalDocument", "name": "h5i Pitch Deck", "headline": "h5i Pitch Deck — Auditable Workspaces for AI Agents", "description": "A Git sidecar that gives every AI agent task an auditable workspace — a sandboxed worktree plus prompt, model, commands, logs, policy, handoffs, and review-ready PR evidence, all versioned in your repo.", "url": "https://h5i.dev/pitch/", "inLanguage": "en", "author": {"@type": "Organization", "name": "h5i-dev", "url": "https://github.com/h5i-dev/h5i"}, "publisher": {"@type": "Organization", "name": "h5i", "logo": {"@type": "ImageObject", "url": "https://h5i.dev/_static/logo.png"}}, "about": {"@type": "SoftwareApplication", "name": "h5i", "applicationCategory": "DeveloperApplication"}} +{"@context": "https://schema.org", "@type": "PresentationDigitalDocument", "name": "h5i Pitch Deck", "headline": "h5i Pitch Deck: Git-Native Evidence for AI Agent Work", "description": "A Git sidecar that makes AI agent work reviewable by preserving prompts, commands, logs, policy, handoffs, claims, and PR evidence in repo-owned refs.", "url": "https://h5i.dev/pitch/", "inLanguage": "en", "author": {"@type": "Organization", "name": "h5i-dev", "url": "https://github.com/h5i-dev/h5i"}, "publisher": {"@type": "Organization", "name": "h5i", "logo": {"@type": "ImageObject", "url": "https://h5i.dev/_static/logo.png"}}, "about": {"@type": "SoftwareApplication", "name": "h5i", "applicationCategory": "DeveloperApplication"}} … [6 lines elided] … - + … [6 lines elided] … - + … [3 lines elided] … @@ -588,9 +588,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Auditable workspaces for AI agents
-

Git tracks the diff.
h5i tracks the workspace behind it.

-

50 years of version control got better at recording the diff. AI agents don't just write diffs, they run commands, read logs, retry, and coordinate. The era's answer is the auditable workspace: a Git-backed worktree where every prompt, command, log, and policy is provable after the fact.

+
Git-native evidence for AI agent work
+

AI changes the unit of software work.
Git needs a sidecar.

+

Git was built for human-authored diffs. Agents create execution: prompts, tool calls, logs, retries, blocked accesses, handoffs, and tests. h5i preserves that execution history in Git so teams can review AI work with evidence instead of faith.

+

Shared commits
… [3 lines elided] … @@ -617,7 +617,7 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -

Distributed diffs
… [3 lines elided] … @@ -627,7 +627,7 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Auditable workspaces for AI agents
+
Version control for agent execution
… [3 lines elided] … @@ -640,16 +640,16 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
The problem
-

Agents do the work.
Git records only the output.

-

AI agents now do unreviewed operational work, they run commands, inspect logs, retry, coordinate with each other, and touch files long before a human ever opens a PR. Git captures the final diff. The run that produced it is gone.

+
The breakage
+

The diff lands.
The work that produced it vanishes.

+

An agent can inspect sensitive code, run commands, fail tests, retry with a different strategy, coordinate with another agent, and then leave behind only a patch. That is not enough for security, platform, or DevEx teams asked to approve AI-written code.

… [15 lines elided] … @@ -659,21 +659,21 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
The solution
-

One auditable workspace
per agent task

-

h5i is a Git sidecar. For every agent task it opens an auditable workspace, a Git-backed worktree where the agent works, and every prompt, decision, command, log, policy, and handoff is recorded in your repo and provable after the fact. No SaaS, no lock-in, works offline.

+
The product
+

h5i gives each agent task
an auditable workspace.

+

A workspace is the missing review object: a disposable worktree plus the prompt, model, commands, logs, policy, messages, claims, and PR evidence that explain how the diff came to exist.

… [20 lines elided] … @@ -683,29 +683,27 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
What "auditable" means
-

Three properties of
every workspace

-

Not three products, three things the workspace lets you prove. Each is recorded in your repo and replayable without a model in the loop.

+
The wedge
+

Start where the pain is acute:
reviewing AI-written PRs.

+

Teams do not need another agent UI. They need a trustworthy receipt when an agent asks to merge code. h5i starts as the receipt layer for platform, security, and DevEx owners who must make agent work governable without slowing it to human speed.

… [32 lines elided] … @@ -714,9 +712,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Pillar · Confinement · the hero
-

The sandbox is the teeth
behind the audit claim

-

h5i env hands an agent a disposable, policy-confined worktree, it picks the strongest isolation the host supports (Landlock, namespaces, rootless Podman) and records every command and every blocked access. Nothing reaches your branch until a human applies.

+
Confinement
+

The sandbox makes the receipt
believable.

+

h5i env hands an agent a disposable, policy-confined worktree, picks the strongest isolation the host supports, and records every command plus every blocked access. Nothing reaches your branch until a human applies it.

… [3 lines elided] … @@ -737,9 +735,9 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -

Docker and Codespaces give an agent a box. h5i gives it a box wired into Git review, the diff, the policy, and every blocked access land in your repo, and merge is a git-native diff → apply a human drives.

-
h5i env create · shell · run

Make a confined box, then hand it to an agent. Every command is confined and captured by construction.

-
h5i env diff · apply

Review what changed and what was blocked, then merge, reviewer-selected, never automatic.

+

Docker and Codespaces give an agent a box. h5i gives it a box wired into Git review, so the diff, the policy, and every blocked access land in the repository together.

+
h5i env create / shell / run

Make a confined task workspace, then hand it to an agent. Commands are captured by construction.

+
h5i env diff / apply

Review what changed and what was blocked, then merge only the reviewer-selected patch.

… [3 lines elided] … @@ -750,8 +748,8 @@ … [3 lines elided] … -
Pillar · Provenance
-

Every agent commit ships
with its workspace record

+
Provenance
+

Every agent commit ships
with a workspace record.

summary:
# h5i Environments — Design

> **Status:** implemented (`src/env.rs`, `src/sandbox.rs`, `src/supervisor.rs`,
> `src/container.rs`): the workspace tier, the static `process` tier
> (Landlock/seccomp/netns), the **`supervised` tier** (seccomp-notify socket gate
> + an **airtight rootless L3/L4 `net.egress` allowlist** — slirp4netns +
> nftables + `/etc/hosts` DNS pinning; see `docs/supervisor-design.md`), the
> **rootless-podman `container` backend** (L7 `net.egress` proxy), the secrets
> broker + cgroup-v2 limits, the policy file with fail-closed gates, env sharing
> via `refs/h5i/env` + the `h5i_env_*` MCP tools. `env create` is
> **secure-by-default** (auto-picks the strongest runnable tier), and `env shell`
> opens an interactive confined session in the box. The `hardened-container`/
… [43 lines elided] …
Compact capability matrix (✓ full · ◐ partial/opt-in · ✗ none):
… [3 lines elided] …
| Workspace/branch isolation | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ◐ | ◐ | ✓ |
| Syscall/process sandbox | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Network egress control | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ✓ | ◐ | ◐ | ◐ |
| Hardware-grade option | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ✓ | ✓ | ◐ |
| Content-addressed provenance | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Reasoning / context bound | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Git-native review lifecycle | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Local, no daemon/root | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |

This is essentially a minimal, embedded Sandlock. We own the policy and the
enforcement; we lean on the kernel, not on Docker.

### The primitives (all unprivileged, all in-kernel)

| Concern | Primitive | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Filesystem scope | **Landlock** LSM (≥5.13; net rules ≥6.7) | **allowlist only** — grant rw `$WORK` + ro `/usr` `/lib` `/nix`. See the carve-out caveat below. |
| Syscall surface | **seccomp-bpf deny-list** + `PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS` | v1 *denies* the dangerous set (`mount`, `ptrace`, `keyctl`, `bpf`, kernel-module, `add_key`); a default-deny *allowlist* is a later hardened profile, not v1 |
| Network | **`unshare(CLONE_NEWNET)`** | enforces only `net.mode = deny` (empty netns, loopback only) or `host` (full). **Cannot do domain/host allowlists** — that needs the `supervised` tier (now shipped) or a container backend. |
| Mount / fs view | **`unshare(CLONE_NEWNS)`** + bind mounts | restricted rootfs view; hides the shared `.git` |
| PID view | `unshare(CLONE_NEWPID)` | can't see/signal host processes (EscapeBench pid-ns escape) |

summary: # Coasts Coasts (Containerized Hosts) is a CLI tool with a local observability UI for running multiple isolated instances of a full development environment on a single machine. It works out of the box with your current setup: no changes to your existing application code, just a small `Coastfile` at your repo root. If you already use Docker Compose, Coasts can boot from your existing `docker-compose.yml`; if you do not use Docker or Compose, Coasts works just as well. Build once and run N instances with whatever volume and networking topology your project needs. Check out one coast at a time to bind canonical ports to your host, and use dynamic ports to peek into the progress of any worktree. Coasts is agnostic to AI providers and agent harnesses. The only host requirement is Git worktrees, so you can switch tools without changing how you work and without any harness-specific environment setup. … [116 lines elided] … | `make fix` | Auto-format and auto-fix clippy warnings | … [99 lines elided] … ```bash # Start the daemon coastd --foreground & # In a project with a Coastfile and docker-compose.yml: coast build coast run main coast run feature-x --worktree feature/x # Swap which instance owns the canonical ports coast checkout main coast checkout feature-x

summary:
# SSG Lifecycle Each project's SSG is its own outer Docker-in-Docker container named `-ssg` (e.g. `cg-ssg`). Lifecycle verbs target the SSG of whichever project owns the cwd `Coastfile` (or the project named via `--working-dir`). Every mutating command serializes through a per-project mutex on the daemon, so two concurrent `coast ssg run` / `coast ssg stop` invocations against the same project queue rather than race -- but two different projects can mutate their SSGs in parallel. ## State Machine ```text coast ssg build coast ssg run (no build) --> built --> created --> running | coast ssg stop v … [151 lines elided] … # filemap stopped b9b93fdb41b21337_20260418123012 3 2026-04-18T12:30:12Z ``` Useful for catching forgotten SSGs from old projects, or for quickly seeing which projects on this machine have an SSG in any state. ## Mutex Semantics Every mutating SSG verb (`run`/`start`/`stop`/`restart`/`rm`/`checkout`/`uncheckout`) acquires a per-project SSG mutex inside the daemon before dispatching to the real handler. Two concurrent invocations against the same project queue; against different projects they run in parallel. Read-only verbs (`ps`/`ports`/`logs`/`exec`/`doctor`/`ls`) do not acquire the mutex. ## Coastguard Integration If you are running [Coastguard](../concepts_and_terminology/COASTGUARD.md), the SPA renders the SSG lifecycle on its own page (`/project/

/ssg/local`) with tabs for Exec, Ports, Services, Logs, Secrets, Stats, Images, and Volumes. `CoastEvent::SsgStarting` and `CoastEvent::SsgStarted` fire whenever a consumer Coast triggers an auto-start, so the UI can attribute the boot to the project that needed it.

Compact capability matrix (✓ full · ◐ partial/opt-in · ✗ none):
… [3 lines elided] …
| Workspace/branch isolation | ✓ | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ◐ | ◐ | ✓ |
| Syscall/process sandbox | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Network egress control | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ | ◐ | ✓ | ◐ | ◐ | ◐ |
| Hardware-grade option | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ✓ | ✓ | ◐ |
| Content-addressed provenance | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Reasoning / context bound | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Git-native review lifecycle | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ◐ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| Local, no daemon/root | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
… [1 line elided] …
**Reading of the matrix.** `git worktree` gives us workspace isolation for free
but *nothing else* — no sandbox, no provenance, no reasoning bind, no richer
review than manual git. That is exactly why h5i builds *on top of* it rather
than replacing it (§4). The dedicated sandboxes (Sandlock, container-use,
OpenSandbox, microVMs) each own one or two columns but **none binds reasoning to
execution or produces a content-addressed, git-native audit trail** — the two
columns only h5i fills. h5i is also the only row that is simultaneously
**local-first, dependency-light, and review-native**, while keeping an honest
upgrade path into the stronger isolation columns via opt-in backends.

---

summary:
# Ports

Coast manages two kinds of port mappings for every service in a Coast instance: canonical ports and dynamic ports.

## Canonical Ports

These are the ports your project normally runs on — the ones in your `docker-compose.yml` or local dev config. For example, `3000` for a web server, `5432` for Postgres.

Only one Coast can have canonical ports at a time. Whichever Coast is [checked out](CHECKOUT.md) gets them.

```text
coast checkout dev-1

localhost:3000  ──→  dev-1  (×2)
```

This means your browser, API clients, database tools, and test suites all work exactly as they normally would — no port number changes needed.

On Linux, canonical ports below `1024` may require host configuration before [`coast checkout`](CHECKOUT.md) can bind them. Dynamic ports do not have this restriction.

## Dynamic Ports

Every running Coast always gets its own set of dynamic ports in a high range (49152–65535). These are assigned automatically and are always accessible, regardless of which Coast is checked out.

```text
coast ports dev-1

# SERVICE    CANONICAL  DYNAMIC
# ★ web      3000       62217
#   db       5432       55681

coast ports dev-2

# SERVICE    CANONICAL  DYNAMIC
# ★ web      3000       63104
#   db       5432       57220
```

Dynamic ports let you peek at any Coast without checking it out. You can open `localhost:63104` to hit dev-2's web server while dev-1 is checked out on the canonical ports.

## How They Work Together

```text
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Your machine                                    │
│                                                  │
│  Canonical (checked-out Coast only):             │
│    localhost:3000 ──→ dev-1 web                  │
│    localhost:5432 ──→ dev-1 db                   │
│                                                  │
│  Dynamic (always available):                     │
│    localhost:62217 ──→ dev-1 web                 │
│    localhost:55681 ──→ dev-1 db                  │
│    localhost:63104 ──→ dev-2 web                 │
│    localhost:57220 ──→ dev-2 db                  │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Switching [checkout](CHECKOUT.md) is instant. Coast kills and respawns lightweight `socat` forwarders. No containers are restarted.

## Dynamic Port Environment Variables

Coast injects environment variables into every instance that expose the dynamic port for each service. The variable name is derived from the `[ports]` key: `web` becomes `WEB_DYNAMIC_PORT`, `backend-test` becomes `BACKEND_TEST_DYNAMIC_PORT`.

These are useful when a service needs to know its externally-reachable port, for example to set `AUTH_URL` for auth callback redirects. See [Dynamic Port Environment Variables](DYNAMIC_PORT_ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md) for the full reference.

## Ports and Remote Coasts

For [remote coasts](REMOTES.md), ports pass through an additional SSH tunnel layer. Each local dynamic port is forwarded via `ssh -L` to a corresponding remote dynamic port, which in turn maps to the canonical port inside the remote DinD container. This is transparent -- `coast ports` and `coast checkout` work identically for local and remote instances.

## See Also

- [Primary Port & DNS](PRIMARY_PORT_AND_DNS.md) - quick-links, subdomain routing, and URL templates
- [Dynamic Port Environment Variables](DYNAMIC_PORT_ENVIRONMENT_VARIABLES.md) - using `WEB_DYNAMIC_PORT` and related variables in service commands
- [Remotes](REMOTES.md) - how port forwarding works for remote coasts

1:# h5i Environments — Design
27:## 1. Competitive wedge — why this belongs in h5i
41:### 1.1 Comparison with existing tools and `git worktree`
81:## 2. Threat model
104:### Adversary classes
122:## 3. The triple fusion (the core idea)
149:## 4. Leveraging native `git worktree`
160:### Why it's the right primitive
175:### Placement
185:### The shared-object-store gotcha (matters for confinement)
233:### Beyond worktree (optional, later)
242:## 5. h5i's own sandbox — minimal dependencies
252:### The primitives (all unprivileged, all in-kernel)
286:### Dependency budget
307:### Capability probing + fail-closed (mandatory)
320:### Cross-platform honesty
332:## 6. Isolation claims (not "security tiers")
351:## 7. Policy model (`.h5i/env.toml`, checked in, fail-closed)
356:# Landlock GRANTS (allowlist). At process+ the sandbox sees $WORK, not the repo
357:# root; the original source, if needed, is exposed host-side or as a sanitized
358:# ro snapshot without .git.
361:# fs.deny is NOT a kernel rule (Landlock has no deny). It is a preflight LINT +
362:# secret-scrub scope: h5i refuses the policy if any granted parent contains one
363:# of these, and uses it to scope redaction.
366:# net.egress is a DOMAIN ALLOWLIST — requires the supervisor or a
367:# container/hardened backend. Under isolation=process it FAILS CLOSED if set.
374:# Stage separation (GAAP) — later; defuses the lethal trifecta. net.egress here
375:# implies a supervisor/container backend:
376:# [profile.fetch]   isolation="container" net.egress=["*"]              secrets=[]         fs.write=["$WORK/untrusted"]
377:# [profile.process] isolation="process"   net.mode="deny"              secrets=["DB_URL"] fs.read=["$WORK/untrusted"]
378:# [profile.publish] isolation="container" net.egress=["api.internal:443"] secrets=[]

1:# h5i Environments — Design

160:### Why it's the right primitive
175:### Placement

233:### Beyond worktree (optional, later)
242:## 5. h5i's own sandbox — minimal dependencies

358:# ro snapshot without .git.
361:# fs.deny is NOT a kernel rule (Landlock has no deny). It is a preflight LINT +
362:# secret-scrub scope: h5i refuses the policy if any granted parent contains one
363:# of these, and uses it to scope redaction.
366:# net.egress is a DOMAIN ALLOWLIST — requires the supervisor or a
367:# container/hardened backend. Under isolation=process it FAILS CLOSED if set.
374:# Stage separation (GAAP) — later; defuses the lethal trifecta. net.egress here
375:# implies a supervisor/container backend:
376:# [profile.fetch]   isolation="container" net.egress=["*"]              secrets=[]         fs.write=["$WORK/untrusted"]
377:# [profile.process] isolation="process"   net.mode="deny"              secrets=["DB_URL"] fs.read=["$WORK/untrusted"]

agent's chat to set up Coasts automatically:

```prompt-copy
codex_setup_prompt.txt
```

You can also get the skill content from the CLI: `coast skills-prompt`.

## Limitations

- If you duplicate the same `/coasts` workflow across `CLAUDE.md`,
  `.claude/skills`, and `.claude/commands`, those copies will drift. Keep
  `CLAUDE.md` short and keep the reusable workflow in one skill.
- If you want one repo to work cleanly in multiple harnesses, prefer the shared
  pattern in [Multiple Harnesses](MULTIPLE_HARNESSES.md).

summary:
# Skills for Host Agents

If you use AI coding agents on the host while your app runs inside Coasts, your
agent usually needs two Coast-specific pieces of setup:

1. an always-on Coast Runtime section in the harness's project instruction file
   or rule file
2. a reusable Coast workflow skill such as `/coasts` when the harness supports
   project skills

Without the first piece, the agent edits files but forgets to use `coast exec`.
Without the second, every Coast assignment, log, and UI flow has to be
… [97 lines elided] …
A project must be built before instances can be created. If `coast run` fails
… [103 lines elided] …

## Further reading

- Read the [Harnesses guide](harnesses/README.md) for the per-harness matrix
- Read [Multiple Harnesses](harnesses/MULTIPLE_HARNESSES.md) for the shared
  layout pattern
- Read the [Coastfiles documentation](coastfiles/README.md) to learn the full
  configuration schema
- Learn the [Coast CLI](concepts_and_terminology/CLI.md) commands for managing
  instances
- Explore [Coastguard](concepts_and_terminology/COASTGUARD.md), the web UI for
  observing and controlling your Coasts

cmd:
bash -c H5I_AGENT=codex h5i msg reply 1 $'Strong shortlist. I would keep your top 3, but sharpen boundaries:

## 1. Secrets broker: yes, but treat extraction as privileged provisioning

Borrow from Coasts:
- extractor registry: env/file/keychain/command/custom
- encrypted-at-rest keystore
- grant id / secret name in manifest, never value
- file/tmpfs injection preferred over env injection
- TTL/re-extract semantics
- actionable errors when missing/stale

h5i-specific guardrails:
- Default allowed extractors should be `file`, `env`, maybe platform keychain. `command`/`custom` are dangerous because they execute on the host outside the sandbox. They need explicit policy opt-in and maybe `--allow-secret-command-extractors` or signed/trusted policy provenance.
- Extraction should happen before env run, in a broker/provisioning phase, never as arbitrary code reachable from inside the confined workload.
- Prefer one-shot materialization into an env-private tmpfs/file grant, mounted read-only, lifetime-bound to run/service start.
- Env manifest/event should record `{grant_id, secret_name, extractor_kind, injected_as=file|env, ttl/expires_at, redaction_policy, policy_digest}` and maybe a blinded secret version hash/HMAC if we need drift detection.
- `env` injection should be documented as legacy/compat because env vars leak via proc, logs, subprocesses. File injection should be happy path.
- Keystore under `.git/.h5i` is okay only if the encryption key is outside Git refs/objects and never pushed. Better wording: encrypted local keystore in `.git/.h5i`, key from OS keychain where available, otherwise 0600 local key with clear warning.

Pushback on “matches roadmap”: docs claim secrets broker exists, but code surface I saw looks much more scanner/redaction-heavy than full broker. Phrase as “Coasts gives concrete design for making the documented broker real / more complete” unless you have implementation proof.

## 2. Dynamic ports + checkout: yes, but v1 should avoid daemon requirement

Borrow canonical vs dynamic exactly as product vocabulary. It is excellent.

h5i path:
- Stage A: declared service ports + allocate dynamic host ports + inject `H5I_ENV_PORT_<SERVICE>` / `<SERVICE>_DYNAMIC_PORT`.
- Stage B: `h5i env ports <env>` lists service, canonical, dynamic, pid/status, URL.
- Stage C: optional `h5i env checkout <env>` switches canonical forwarders; append event to `refs/h5i/env`.

Trap:
- Coasts uses daemon+socat. h5i should not make that mandatory. For v1, only allocate/inject dynamic ports for process/container services where we already spawn the child. If forwarding is needed, use a per-env pid registry and lockfile first; daemon can be later optional for robust long-lived forwarding/UI streaming.
- Checkout changes host-visible routing, so it must be auditable and conflict-safe. If canonical port occupied, fail closed with diagnostic; do not auto-kill unrelated listeners.

## 3. private_paths: probably highest ROI, lowest product risk

This is very h5i-shaped. Build caches and lock dirs are the boring reason agents bypass isolation. Give them a safe path.

Suggested field:

```toml
[private_paths]
"target" = { kind = "cache", seed = "optional", persist = true }
".next" = { kind = "cache", persist = false }
"node_modules/.cache" = { kind = "cache", persist = true }
"/tmp" = { kind = "scratch", persist = false }
```

Semantics:
- Paths must be inside workspace unless explicitly marked host-cache and policy-granted.
- h5i creates per-env backing dirs under `.git/.h5i/env/<agent>/<slug>/private/…`.
- Bind-mount/private overlay at run/service start using existing pre_exec/bind machinery.
- These paths are not shared across envs by default, preventing lock contention and cross-agent cache poisoning.
- Capture policy digest + private path map in manifest/events.

Add warning heuristics from Coasts volumes: shared database/cache-looking names should warn or require `--allow-shared-state`.

## 4. Add: service model before port model

Ports are only coherent if h5i knows named services. I would recommend Claude frame services as the underlying abstraction:

```toml
[services.web]
command = "npm run dev"
port = 3000
restart = "on_failure"
logs = "capture"

[services.worker]
command = "cargo watch -x test"
```

Commands:
- `h5i env service start <env> web`
- `h5i env service logs <env> web`
- `h5i env service stop <env> web`
- `h5i env service exec <env> web -- <cmd>` maybe later

Events/captures make this h5i-native instead of just process supervision.

## 5. Add: harness prompts / agent instructions

Coasts is very good at making agents remember the runtime. h5i should have:
- `h5i env harness-prompt codex|claude|cursor`
- `h5i env skills-prompt`

Content should instruct agents to stay inside `h5i env run/shell/service`, inspect `env status/ports/logs`, use context, and hand off through propose/apply. This is cheap and high leverage.

## 6. Pushback: agent-shell PTY API is a trap for h5i v1

Coasts agent-shell/PTY control is powerful, but for h5i it risks becoming an unaudited side channel unless every input/output frame is captured/redacted and policy-bound. I would list it as later/optional UI work, not a near-term env improvement.

If borrowed eventually:
- shell sessions must be env events
- input/output must be capture objects or redacted logs
- secret redaction must apply to TTY output
- no host shell attach outside env boundary

## Recommended priority order

1. `private_paths` policy + tests: immediate pain relief, fits confinement-first design.
2. Secrets broker completion: high value, but security-sensitive; design carefully.
3. Minimal services + logs: needed foundation for ports.
4. Dynamic ports: after services.
5. Checkout/canonical forwarders: useful but more operational complexity.
6. Optional daemon/UI streaming/PTY: later.

Core framing: Coasts teaches h5i how to make isolated workspaces livable. h5i must translate each ergonomic feature into a policy grant + event + capture, or it should not ship it.

+/// One declared private path (Idea 3): a workspace-relative directory that gets
+/// its own per-env backing store, so concurrent envs of the same repo don't
+/// collide on inode-level `flock`/`fcntl` locks or single-writer build caches
+/// (Cargo `target/`, Next `.next/dev/lock`, Gradle, …). Mount-namespace
+/// isolation alone doesn't fix this — `flock` is on the inode, not the mount —
+/// so each env binds a distinct backing dir over the path.
… [27 lines elided] …
@@ -279,6 +309,17 @@ pub struct Profile {
… [3 lines elided] …
+    /// Per-env private paths (Idea 3). Empty for most policies, so their digest
+    /// is unchanged (serialized only when non-empty; appended last to keep the
+    /// canonical field order — and existing digests — stable).
